does: This paper introduces Semi-Supervised Reward Modeling (SSRM), a method that trains reward models for language model alignment using both labeled and unlabeled preference data. The approach iteratively pseudo-labels unlabeled examples, filters predictions by confidence threshold to retain high-confidence samples, and performs supervised finetuning on the refined dataset. The authors conduct extensive experiments across different model configurations and demonstrate that SSRM achieves performance comparable to fully supervised reward models trained on equivalent volumes of labeled data, substantially reducing the annotation burden required for RLHF systems.
connects: This work builds on the established pipeline of Reinforcement Learning with Human Feedback (RLHF) for aligning large language models, which depends critically on high-quality reward models trained from human preference judgments. It extends recent efforts to reduce annotation costs in preference learning by combining ideas from semi-supervised learning with reward modeling. The paper responds to the scalability bottleneck in RLHF systems, drawing on self-training techniques that have proven effective in other machine learning domains. It connects to prior work on efficient preference learning and confidence-based sample selection strategies that have been developed for reducing labeling requirements.
enables: This approach opens avenues for more scalable RLHF systems that can leverage abundant unlabeled preference data or model-generated comparisons without requiring exhaustive human annotation. It points toward hybrid training pipelines that mix human-annotated and self-trained signals, potentially enabling deployment of aligned language models with significantly reduced human oversight costs. The work suggests future directions in combining semi-supervised techniques with other efficiency methods in preference learning, and may inspire similar approaches for other components of the alignment pipeline. It could facilitate alignment research in resource-constrained settings and enable rapid iteration on large-scale language models.
